message.content:
You are a senior applied cryptographer doing a DEEP second-pass audit of Coinbase cb-mpc. A first broad pass found nothing; go deeper and more exhaustive on this narrow surface. Rigor over volume.
REPO ROOT: C:/bugBounty/targets/cb-mpc. Read the named files IN FULL; follow into include/cbmpc/** and include-internal/** for definitions. Anchor every finding to a public API entry (ecdsa_mp.h, pve_*.h message round-trips).

BOUNTY SCOPE: eligible only if reachable via public APIs in include/cbmpc/api/. demo-* and include/cbmpc/c_api/* are OUT OF SCOPE as entry points. Primitive/ZK bugs eligible only when tied to a named public-API path. Medium+ needs an independent-parties PoC (>=1 honest party on unmodified code; malicious party interacts only through the public protocol boundary). Tiers: Critical $50k (key compromise/RCE), High $15k, Medium $2k, Low $200.

SURFACE: Deserialization of mp-ECDSA / PVE protocol messages from an untrusted counterparty. From the protocol files trace how received messages are parsed (src/cbmpc/core/convert.cpp, buf*.cpp and any per-message deserialize). Hunt missing length/bounds checks, integer overflow, OOB reads, identity/zero points accepted, that lead to crash (Low) or memory corruption / logic bypass (higher) reachable from a public-API round.

Build an explicit mental table: received-value -> is it validated? where? if not, attack. Report ONLY values used without a required check that a malicious party can exploit with honest counterparties on unmodified code.
At most 5 findings, best-first; ZERO is a fully acceptable, honest result. Each finding: public_api_entry, exact file:line, the precise missing_check, data_flow with function names, attacker_role, impact, poc_sketch through the public API, honest confidence in [0,1].
In coverage_notes, briefly list the received values you DID confirm are properly validated (evidence the pass was thorough). Return ONLY the structured object.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- contribute:contribute: This skill should be used when the user asks to "contribute to open source", "find an issue to work on", "analyze a repo", "submit a pull request", "review PR feedback", "triage an issue", "sync my fork", "create a release", "clean up contribution branches", "debug CI failures", or mentions open-source contribution workflows. Provides a full-lifecycle open-source contribution system with 12 phases.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: The PVE protocol looks well-validated. The deserialization is the key surface. Let me find the convert.h and the ec_pve_t serialization (the message round-trip), and how ecc_point_t / bn_t deserialize.

content.text: Now I see the deserialization of `ec_pve_t`. The key observation: `convert(Q, L, b)` deserializes `Q` as an `ecc_point_t`. Let me look at how `ecc_point_t::convert` deserializes — is the point validated to be on the curve at deserialization time, or only later? And crucially, in `verify()`, line 73 calls `curve.check(Q)` on the *parameter* Q, but the parameter Q passed in `decrypt` is `this->Q` (line 150 passes `Q` member). Let me check ecc_point_t convert and bn_t convert.

input.command: cd /c/bugBounty/targets/cb-mpc && grep -rn 'void convert' include/cbmpc/crypto/base*.h include-internal/cbmpc/internal/crypto/*.h 2>/dev/null | grep -iE 'ecc_point|ecc_generator|bn_t|ec_pve' ; echo '---ecpoint conv impl---'; grep -rn 'ecc_point_t::convert\|ecc_point_t::from_bin\|to_compressed\|from_compressed' src/cbmpc/crypto/*.cpp | head -40

content.text: Point deserialization calls `curve.check(*this)` — validates on-curve. Good. Let me check `curve.check` whether it rejects the identity/infinity point, and the `ecurve_t::convert` (does it accept arbitrary curve id from attacker?). Also check `from_bin` for secp256k1 to verify identity handling.

The point deserialization is robust: validates curve, on-curve, subgroup, and rejects infinity. The `b` field is a `buf128_t` (fixed 16 bytes), `r[i]` is `buf128_t`. `x_rows[i]` are `bn_t` (validated in verify against range). `c[i]` are `buf_t` (length-prefixed, bounded by MAX_CONVERT_LEN). 

Now the crucial question for PVE: in `decrypt`, `Q` deserialized is validated in `verify` (curve.check). But note `restore_from_decrypted` uses `b.get_bit(row_index)` — `b` is buf128_t fixed size, kappa=SEC_P_COM. Let me check the public PVE API entry to confirm the deserialize path and how `curve` is supplied to decrypt vs the deserialized Q's curve. Let me check the public api pve files.

content.content:
1	#pragma once
2	
3	#include <cbmpc/api/curve.h>
4	#include <cbmpc/core/buf.h>
5	#include <cbmpc/core/error.h>
6	
7	namespace coinbase::api::pve {
8	
9	// Pluggable base public-key encryption (PKE) used by PVE.
10	//
11	// The PVE construction requires *deterministic* encryption given `rho`:
12	// calling `encrypt(...)` with the same inputs (including the same `rho`) must
13	// produce the same ciphertext.
14	//
15	// Implementations must treat `rho` as the sole source of encryption randomness.
16	class base_pke_i {
17	 public:
18	  virtual ~base_pke_i() = default;
19	
20	  // Encrypt `plain` under `ek` with associated data `label`, using `rho` as
21	  // deterministic randomness seed.
22	  virtual error_t encrypt(mem_t ek, mem_t label, mem_t plain, mem_t rho, buf_t& out_ct) const = 0;
23	
24	  // Decrypt `ct` under `dk` with associated data `label`.
25	  virtual error_t decrypt(mem_t dk, mem_t label, mem_t ct, buf_t& out_plain) const = 0;
26	};
27	
28	// Built-in base PKE implementation (default).
29	//
30	// Key format:
31	// - `ek` / `dk` are opaque, versioned byte strings generated by:
32	//   - `generate_base_pke_rsa_keypair(...)`
33	//   - `generate_base_pke_ecies_p256_keypair(...)`
34	//
35	// Notes:
36	// - The built-in base PKE supports RSA-OAEP(2048) and ECIES(P-256).
37	// - The base PKE is independent of the curve used by the outer PVE scheme.
38	const base_pke_i& base_pke_default();
39	
40	// Generate a keypair for the built-in RSA-OAEP (2048-bit) base PKE.
41	//
42	// Outputs are opaque, versioned byte strings compatible with `base_pke_default()`.
43	error_t generate_base_pke_rsa_keypair(buf_t& out_ek, buf_t& out_dk);
44	
45	// Generate a keypair for the built-in ECIES (P-256) base PKE.
46	//
47	// Outputs are opaque, versioned byte strings compatible with `base_pke_default()`.
48	error_t generate_base_pke_ecies_p256_keypair(buf_t& out_ek, buf_t& out_dk);
49	
50	// Build a built-in ECIES(P-256) base PKE public key blob from an external public key.
51	//
52	// This is useful when the private key lives in an HSM (or other external system)
53	// and only the public key can be exported to software.
54	//
55	// Input format:
56	// - `pub_key_oct` must be the *uncompressed* NIST P-256 public key octet string:
57	//   65 bytes: 0x04 || X(32) || Y(32).
58	//
59	// Output:
60	// - `out_ek` is an opaque, versioned byte string compatible with:
61	//   - `base_pke_default()`
62	//   - `encrypt/verify(...)`
63	//   - `decrypt_ecies_p256_hsm(...)`
64	error_t base_pke_ecies_p256_ek_from_oct(mem_t pub_key_oct, buf_t& out_ek);
65	
66	// Build a built-in RSA-OAEP(2048) base PKE public key blob from a raw modulus.
67	//
68	// This is useful when the private key lives in an HSM (or other external system)
69	// that exports only the raw modulus (e.g. YubiHSM 2).
70	//
71	// Input format:
72	// - `modulus` must be the big-endian RSA modulus (256 bytes for RSA-2048).
73	// - The public exponent is assumed to be 65537.
74	//
75	// Output:
76	// - `out_ek` is an opaque, versioned byte string compatible with:
77	//   - `base_pke_default()`
78	//   - `encrypt/verify(...)`
79	//   - `decrypt_rsa_oaep_hsm(...)`
80	error_t base_pke_rsa_ek_from_modulus(mem_t modulus, buf_t& out_ek);
81	
82	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
83	// Built-in HSM support (KEM decapsulation callback)
84	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
85	
86	// RSA-OAEP decapsulation callback.
87	//
88	// This callback is responsible only for recovering the RSA KEM shared secret
89	// from `kem_ct` using an HSM-held private key referenced by `dk_handle`.
90	// The library performs HKDF + AES-GCM DEM and ciphertext parsing.
91	struct rsa_oaep_hsm_decap_cb_t {
92	  void* ctx = nullptr;
93	  error_t (*decap)(void* ctx, mem_t dk_handle, mem_t kem_ct, buf_t& out_kem_ss) = nullptr;
94	};
95	
96	// ECIES(P-256) ECDH callback.
97	//
98	// This callback should perform the ECDH operation using the HSM-held private
99	// key referenced by `dk_handle` and the ephemeral public key encoded in `kem_ct`.
100	// It must output the raw affine-X coordinate as a 32-byte big-endian buffer.
101	// The library derives the final KEM shared secret and performs HKDF + AES-GCM DEM.
102	struct ecies_p256_hsm_ecdh_cb_t {
103	  void* ctx = nullptr;
104	  error_t (*ecdh)(void* ctx, mem_t dk_handle, mem_t kem_ct, buf_t& out_dh_x32) = nullptr;
105	};
106	
107	// Encrypt a scalar `x` under base encryption key `ek`, producing a PVE ciphertext.
108	//
109	// - `curve` selects the elliptic curve used for the outer PVE relation (Q = x*G).
110	// - `label` is associated data that must match in `verify()` / `decrypt()`.
111	// - `x` is interpreted as a big-endian integer and reduced modulo the curve order.
112	// - Defensive limit: `x.size` must be <= the curve order size in bytes.
113	error_t encrypt(const base_pke_i& base_pke, curve_id curve, mem_t ek, mem_t label, mem_t x, buf_t& out_ciphertext);
114	
115	// Same as `encrypt(...)` using `base_pke_default()`.
116	error_t encrypt(curve_id curve, mem_t ek, mem_t label, mem_t x, buf_t& out_ciphertext);
117	
118	// Verify ciphertext validity against the expected public value Q and label.
119	//
120	// - `Q_compressed` is the compressed public point encoding for `curve`
121	//   (33 bytes for P-256/secp256k1, 32 bytes for Ed25519).
122	error_t verify(const base_pke_i& base_pke, curve_id curve, mem_t ek, mem_t ciphertext, mem_t Q_compressed, mem_t label);
123	
124	// Same as `verify(...)` using `base_pke_default()`.
125	error_t verify(curve_id curve, mem_t ek, mem_t ciphertext, mem_t Q_compressed, mem_t label);
126	
127	// Decrypt a ciphertext, recovering the scalar x.
128	//
129	// Output:
130	// - On success, `out_x` is set to a fixed-length big-endian encoding of x with
131	//   length equal to the curve order size in bytes (e.g., 32 bytes for P-256).
132	//
133	// Notes:
134	// - This function intentionally does not verify `ciphertext` before decryption.
135	//   Invalid ciphertexts may cause decryption to fail, but are designed to not
136	//   leak secret information.
137	// - If you need ciphertext validation, call `verify(...)` first.
138	error_t decrypt(const base_pke_i& base_pke, curve_id curve, mem_t dk, mem_t ek, mem_t ciphertext, mem_t label,
139	                buf_t& out_x);
140	
141	// Same as `decrypt(...)` using `base_pke_default()`.
142	error_t decrypt(curve_id curve, mem_t dk, mem_t ek, mem_t ciphertext, mem_t label, buf_t& out_x);
143	
144	// Decrypt using an HSM-backed RSA private key.
145	//
146	// - `dk_handle` is an opaque handle (or identifier) understood by the caller's HSM callback.
147	// - `ek` is the software public key blob (used for verification and encryption-side checks).
148	error_t decrypt_rsa_oaep_hsm(curve_id curve, mem_t dk_handle, mem_t ek, mem_t ciphertext, mem_t label,
149	                             const rsa_oaep_hsm_decap_cb_t& cb, buf_t& out_x);
150	
151	// Decrypt using an HSM-backed ECIES(P-256) private key.
152	//
153	// - `dk_handle` is an opaque handle (or identifier) understood by the caller's HSM callback.
154	// - `ek` is the software public key blob (used for verification and to derive the KEM context).
155	error_t decrypt_ecies_p256_hsm(curve_id curve, mem_t dk_handle, mem_t ek, mem_t ciphertext, mem_t label,
156	                               const ecies_p256_hsm_ecdh_cb_t& cb, buf_t& out_x);
157	
158	// Extract the public value Q whose discrete log is encrypted as the ciphertext, returning compressed point encoding.
159	error_t get_public_key_compressed(mem_t ciphertext, buf_t& out_Q_compressed);
160	
161	// Extract the associated label from a ciphertext.
162	error_t get_Label(mem_t ciphertext, buf_t& out_label);
163	
164	}  // namespace coinbase::api::pve
165

content.text: This API path is well-guarded. Decrypt path uses `skip_verify=true` but `restore_from_decrypted` does range checks and the final `x_value*G != Q` check. Note that `decrypt` skips verify and relies on `restore_from_decrypted` recomputing — but `b` is attacker controlled (buf128_t fixed 16 bytes = 128 bits, kappa). Let me check the batch PVE path, which often has more complex parsing with vectors. Let me look at pve_batch and pve_ac which have vector deserialization driven by counts.

Important: in `ec_pve_batch_t::convert`, deserialization reads `Q` (a vector) with its own length prefix via `converter.convert(Q,...)`. But `n` is set by the constructor, and the convert checks `Q.size() != n` *before* reading. On read, `convert(Q)` clears and resizes Q to the attacker-supplied count. So the pre-check `Q.size()==n` is on the *initial* (constructor) value. After reading, Q.size() becomes attacker-controlled count, which may differ from `n`. Then `rows[i].x_bin` etc. read. Let me check how `n` relates to rows and whether downstream indexes Q by something tied to n. Let me look at pve_batch.cpp and the batch API entry.

Now the key question for batch: how is `n` set on the deserialization side? The constructor sets `n` from `batch_count`, and convert checks `Q.size()==n` initially. Let me check the batch API entry to see how the object is constructed before deserialization, and whether `n` is derived from the attacker blob or fixed by the caller.

Here is the critical issue. In `ec_pve_batch_t::convert` (pve_batch.h:48), `n` is set from `blob.batch_count` (the version-prefix field, separate from the inner `ct`). The inner `convert(Q, ...)` reads its OWN length-prefixed vector count for `Q`, which is independent of `n`.

The check `if (int(Q.size()) != n)` runs BEFORE `convert(Q,...)`, where Q was resized to `n` by the constructor. So that check always passes. After reading, `Q.size()` becomes whatever the attacker put in the inner Q length prefix — could be != n.

Now trace `restore_from_decrypted` (pve_batch.cpp:181): loops `for i in 0..n`, accessing `Q[i]`. If attacker set inner Q count < n, then `Q[i]` for `i >= Q.size()` is an **out-of-bounds vector read**. But wait — decrypt uses `skip_verify=true`, calls `restore_from_decrypted`. Let me verify there's no check that `Q.size()==n` after deserialization in the decrypt path.

In `decrypt_batch`: constructs `pve_ct(n)`, deserializes, then loops `get_Qs()` to check curve — that iterates `Q` (size = attacker count, fine). Then calls `pve_ct.decrypt(...)` which calls `restore_from_decrypted`, looping `i in 0..n` accessing `Q[i]`. If attacker's inner Q count `< n`, **OOB read on `Q[i]`** at pve_batch.cpp:183.

But to reach `restore_from_decrypted`, the base_pke.decrypt must succeed for some row. Let me confirm `vector_from_bin` and the `x[i]=x0[i]+x1[i]` — x0/x1 sized by n, Q sized by attacker count. Let me verify the convert resizes Q and check `get_Qs` curve loop wouldn't crash. Actually if attacker sets Q count LARGER than n, then curve check loop is fine, decrypt loop reads Q[0..n-1] within bounds. The dangerous case is count < n.

Let me double check: does `decrypt_batch` verify Q.size matches n anywhere? No. And `verify_batch` checks `Qs_compressed.size()==n` but compares against `Q_expected` (size n) vs `this->Q` (attacker size) via `Q != this->Q` which returns mismatch safely. So verify is safe. But **decrypt with skip_verify=true** is the hole.

Let me confirm the constructor assertion vs n from blob and that batch_count is bounded (yes, MAX_BATCH_COUNT). Let me check `vector_from_bin` to confirm x0/x1 have size n.

In `restore_from_decrypted`, x0 and x1 come from `vector_from_bin(..., n, ...)` so they have size `n`. The loop `for i in 0..n: x[i]=x0[i]+x1[i]; if(Q[i]!=...)` — `Q[i]` with `i` up to `n-1` while `Q.size()` is attacker-controlled.

This is a genuine OOB read: `Q` is `std::vector<ecc_point_t>`, so `Q[i]` for i >= size() dereferences an `ecc_point_t` containing an `EC_POINT*` pointer beyond the allocation, then computes `x[i]*G` and compares with that garbage point — calling into OpenSSL with a wild pointer. This is heap OOB read leading to crash or worse.

But let me verify reachability: to reach `restore_from_decrypted`, `base_pke.decrypt(dk, ..., rows[i].c, x_buf)` must succeed (return SUCCESS) for at least one row. The attacker controls `rows[i].c` (ciphertext) AND the honest decryptor holds the real `dk`. For the attacker to make decrypt succeed, they'd need a valid ciphertext under the honest party's `ek`. The attacker knows `ek` (public). So the attacker CAN produce valid base-PKE ciphertexts in `rows[i].c`. So reachability holds: attacker crafts a blob with `batch_count = n` large, but inner serialized `Q` vector with count < n, and valid `c[i]`. 

Let me confirm the convert reads Q count fresh. Yes — `converter.convert(Q,...)` calls the `std::vector<T>` overload which does `value.clear(); convert_len(count); value.resize(count)`. So Q is fully replaced. The earlier `Q.size()!=n` check is meaningless post-read. Confirmed.

Let me also check the AC batch variant quickly for the same pattern, and confirm there's no post-deser size guard I missed.

Confirmed: `verify` (pve_batch.cpp:78) checks `Q.size()!=n`, but `decrypt` does NOT. The decrypt path with `skip_verify=true` never re-validates `Q.size()==n`. So the OOB is real and decrypt-only.

Note `restore_from_decrypted` checks `row_index` bounds but not `Q.size()`. The vulnerable line is the `Q[i]` access in the `for i in 0..n` loop.

Let me confirm there isn't an implicit guard: when `Q.size() < n`, before reaching restore, the `get_Qs()` curve-check loop in decrypt_batch iterates over the (small) Q vector fine. So no early rejection. The attack proceeds.

I have a solid Low/Medium finding (OOB read crash; potentially info disclosure / corruption via wild EC_POINT* deref). Severity: it's an OOB heap read with a wild pointer dereferenced as `EC_POINT*` inside OpenSSL `EC_POINT_cmp`/point mul — could be exploitable beyond crash, but conservatively it's a remotely-triggered crash (DoS) on the honest decryptor. Per the bounty's independent-parties PoC requirement: honest decryptor runs `decrypt_batch` on attacker-supplied ciphertext blob. That satisfies the boundary. I'll rate it Medium with honest confidence ~0.6 (reachability requires a valid base-PKE ciphertext in at least one row, which the attacker can produce using the public `ek`).

Let me verify one more time that the attacker can supply a single valid row ciphertext - the attacker knows ek (it's the recipient's public key, public). Yes. Confidence stands.

findings.title: Batch PVE decrypt: attacker-controlled inner Q vector length is not re-checked against batch_count, causing out-of-bounds std::vector read of ecc_point_t (wild EC_POINT* deref) on the honest decryptor
findings.public_api_entry: coinbase::api::pve::decrypt_batch(curve_id, dk, ek, ciphertext, label, out_xs) in include/cbmpc/api/pve_batch_single_recipient.h
findings.vuln_class: Out-of-bounds read / missing length validation on deserialized container
findings.missing_check: After deserializing ec_pve_batch_t from the untrusted blob, nothing verifies that the inner length-prefixed Q vector (read by ec_pve_batch_t::convert -> converter.convert(Q,...)) has size == n. ec_pve_batch_t::convert (include-internal/cbmpc/internal/protocol/pve_batch.h:48-61) checks `if (int(Q.size()) != n)` BEFORE reading, when Q is still the constructor-sized vector, so the check is a no-op; the subsequent std::vector overload does Q.clear()/resize(attacker_count), making Q.size() fully attacker-controlled. verify() (pve_batch.cpp:78) does enforce Q.size()==n, but the decrypt path runs with skip_verify=true and never re-validates. restore_from_decrypted() validates row_index but not Q.size().
findings.data_flow: decrypt_batch -> parse_batch_ciphertext sets n = blob.batch_count (separate outer field, bounded by MAX_BATCH_COUNT) -> ec_pve_batch_t pve_ct(n) (constructor resizes Q to n) -> coinbase::convert(pve_ct, blob.ct) -> ec_pve_batch_t::convert: pre-check Q.size()==n passes (still n), then converter.convert(Q,...) clears and resizes Q to the attacker's inner length prefix m (m can be < n) -> get_Qs() curve loop iterates only the m present entries (no rejection) -> pve_ct.decrypt(..., skip_verify=true) -> for each kappa row, base_pke.decrypt(dk, inner_label, rows[i].c, x_buf): attacker can craft rows[i].c as a valid base-PKE ciphertext under the public ek so this returns SUCCESS for some row -> restore_from_decrypted(i, x_buf, curve, xs): builds x0,x1 of size n via bn_t::vector_from_bin, then loop `for (int i=0;i<n;i++){ x[i]=x0[i]+x1[i]; if (Q[i] != x[i]*G) ... }` reads Q[i] for i up to n-1 while Q.size()==m<n -> OOB std::vector<ecc_point_t> element whose embedded EC_POINT* pointer is uninitialized/out-of-bounds heap memory, then dereferenced inside ecc_point_t::operator!= (OpenSSL EC_POINT_cmp).
findings.impact: A malicious counterparty who supplies the serialized batch-PVE ciphertext can force the honest party's decrypt_batch (or get_public_keys_compressed_batch / decrypt_batch_*_hsm, which share the same construct+deserialize pattern) to read past the end of the Q vector and dereference a wild EC_POINT* in OpenSSL. At minimum this is a remotely triggerable crash (DoS) of the honest decryptor; because the wild pointer is consumed as an OpenSSL EC_POINT object, the concrete impact ranges from segfault to controlled OOB read depending on heap layout. No honest-party secret is leaked, but it is reachable purely through the public API boundary with valid base-PKE rows.
findings.poc_sketch: 1) Honest party generates (ek,dk) via generate_base_pke_*_keypair and publishes ek. 2) Attacker calls encrypt_batch(curve, ek, label, xs) with xs of size m (e.g. m=1) to obtain a well-formed pve_batch_ciphertext_blob_v1_t whose inner ct serializes a valid ec_pve_batch_t with Q.size()==1 and valid rows[i].c under ek. 3) Attacker edits only the OUTER blob field batch_count from 1 to a larger n (e.g. 50000), leaving the inner ct (Q vector length prefix = 1, valid c rows) unchanged; re-serialize. 4) Honest party calls decrypt_batch(curve, dk, ek, attacker_blob, label, out_xs). parse sets n=50000; constructor sizes Q to 50000; deserialization overwrites Q with the 1-element inner vector; skip_verify decrypt reaches restore_from_decrypted which loops i in [0,50000) reading Q[i] far past the 1 valid element -> OOB read / crash. Honest party runs unmodified code; attacker only crosses the public ciphertext boundary.
findings.attacker_role: Malicious party that produces the batch-PVE ciphertext blob consumed by an honest decryptor via the public decrypt_batch API.

content.content: Structured output provided successfully

content.text:
I completed a deep second-pass audit of the mp-ECDSA and PVE deserialization surface. One finding reported.

Summary of the finding (Medium, confidence 0.6): In the batch-PVE public API (`decrypt_batch` and the construct+deserialize siblings `get_public_keys_compressed_batch`, `decrypt_batch_*_hsm`), the deserialized inner `Q` vector length is never re-validated against the outer `batch_count`. The size guard in `ec_pve_batch_t::convert` (include-internal/cbmpc/internal/protocol/pve_batch.h:48) runs before the vector is read and is therefore a no-op; `verify()` enforces the equality but `decrypt` runs with `skip_verify=true` and skips it. The result is an out-of-bounds `std::vector<ecc_point_t>` read at src/cbmpc/protocol/pve_batch.cpp:183, dereferencing a wild `EC_POINT*` inside OpenSSL on the honest decryptor. Reachable purely through the public ciphertext boundary because the attacker can produce valid base-PKE rows under the published public `ek`.

Coverage notes (received values I confirmed are properly validated):
- `convert.cpp` / `convert.h`: all integer reads bounds-check via `at_least`; `convert_len` caps at `MAX_CONVERT_LEN`; `std::vector`/`std::string` counts capped at `MAX_CONTAINER_ELEMENTS`; `deser` rejects trailing bytes; `forward`/`at_least` overflow-guarded.
- `ecc_point_t::convert_fixed_curve` (base_ecc.cpp:737): deserialized points are length-checked, on-curve checked, subgroup checked, and rejected if infinity via `ecurve_t::check`; curve code is validated through `ecurve_t::find`.
- `ec_pve_t` (non-batch) decrypt/`restore_from_decrypted`: `x_rows[i]` range-checked against q, `decrypted_x_buf.size <= max_scalar_size`, final `x*G==Q` check; `b`/`r[i]` are fixed-size buf128_t; `c[i]` length-bounded.
- `ec_pve_batch_t::verify`: enforces `Q.size()==n`, `rows[i].r` sizes (16/32), `rows[i].x_bin.size()==n*curve_size`.
- mp-ECDSA `sign` (protocol/ecdsa_mp.cpp): `ot_role_map` fully validated for dimensions/values/anti-symmetry; `v_theta._j` range-checked against q; all received commitments/ZK proofs verified; `Q == SUM(Qis)` and `x_share*G==Qi` checked.
- Batch API outer parsing: `batch_count==0` rejected and bounded by `MAX_BATCH_COUNT`; `verify_batch` checks `Qs_compressed.size()==n`.
